coredata.dc:title: Facile green synthesis of fingernails derived carbon quantum dots for Cu2+ sensing and photodegradation of 2,4-dichlorophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Tai, Jun Yan
Leong, Kah Hon
Saravanan, Pichiah
Tan, Sin Tee
Chong, Woon Chan
Sim, Lan Ching
coredata.dc:description:
The present study reports a facile method to prepare carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via hydrothermal treatment using human fingernails as a green precursor. Fingernails derived CQDs (FN-CQDs) could selectively detect copper ions (Cu2+) at the concentration as low as 1 nM. Different weightage of FN-CQDs (30 wt%, 40 wt% and 50 wt%) were coupled with pure graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) to remove 2,4-dicholorophenol (2,4-DCP) under sunlight irradiation. The composite loaded with 50 wt% of FN-CQD removed 100 % of 2,4-DCP in 75 min, which was almost 2 times higher than g-C3N4. The photocatalytic performance was in agreement with ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) in which the photosensitizing effect was significantly exerted by 50 wt% of CQDs. In the presence of FN-CQDs, g-C3N4 was sensitized by sunlight with an average light intensity of ∼ 937 × 100 lx to donate more electrons for the generation of oxidizing radicals. Excessive loading of FN-CQDs (up to 50 wt%) created trap state that decreased the charge carrier transport in FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50). Such drawbacks did not affect the overall performance of FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50). The higher loading of FN-CQDs exerted stronger photosensitizing effects to overcome the limitation of high recombination rate of charge carriers and lower surface area. FN-CQDs could act as photosensitizer to increase the light absorption range to generate more electron and holes. It also served as electron reservoir for the reduction of oxygen molecule to produce superoxide anion radical (
O2 −). Scavenging tests identified that
O2 − was the most active radical in the photodegradation of 2,4-DCP.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM images of (a) pure g-C3N4, (b) FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(30), (c) FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(40), (d) FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50), (e) TEM Image, and (f) HRTEM Image of FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50). The insets of (b) and (e) show EDX result and particle size distribution of FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) and FN-CQDs, respectively (Image obtained at 20 kV (FESEM), 200 kV (TEM)).
FTIR spectra of (a) human fingernails and FN-CQDs, and (b) pure g-C3N4, FN-CQDs/g -C3N4(30), FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(40), and FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) (scanning range = 400 to 4000 cm−1 at 2 cm−1 resolution).

(a) XRD patterns of pure g-C3N4, FN-CQDs, FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(30), FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(40), and FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) (Cu Kα radiation, λ =1.54 Å), and (b) PL emission wavelength of FN-CQDs aqueous solution at different excitation spectrum (λex =325 nm).
(a) UV–vis DRS, and (b) Tauc plot of pure g-C3N4, FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(30), FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(40), and FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) (wavelength range of 200– 800 nm).

(a) Nanosecond-level time resolved fluorescence decay spectra (λex =480 nm), and (b) Transient photocurrent response of pure g-C3N4, FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(30), FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(40), and FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) (Newport AM 1.5 G solar simulator, 10 s interval).
XPS spectra (a) C1s, (b) N1s, and (c) O1s of FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) (Al Kα radiation source).

(a) Fluorescence intensity of FN-CQDs towards the detection of different metal ions, and (b) PL emission of FN-CQDs solution with different Cu2+ concentration (FN-CQDs conc. = 0.1 g/L; metal solution conc. = 1 mM; Cu2+ conc. = 0‒1000 nM).

(a) Photocatalytic performance, (b) Scavenging tests of FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(30), FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(40), FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50), g-C3N4, and blank, and (c) Proposed photocatalysis mechanism (initial conc. of 2,4-DCP =10 mg/L; catalyst dosage = 0.05 g; average light intensity = ∼ 937 × 100 lx (photodegradation), ∼ 958 × 100 lx (scavenging); duration =2 h).
BET surface area, pore volume and average pore size of the prepared samples.
Correlation between physicochemical properties and photodegradation performance of 2,4-DCP.
Comparison study of the degradation of EDCs with various photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Facile green synthesis of fingernails derived carbon quantum dots for Cu2+ sensing and photodegradation of 2,4-dichlorophenol

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The present study reports a facile method to prepare carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via hydrothermal treatment using human fingernails as a green precursor. Fingernails derived CQDs (FN-CQDs) could selectively detect copper ions (Cu2+) at the concentration as low as 1 nM. Different weightage of FN-CQDs (30 wt%, 40 wt% and 50 wt%) were coupled with pure graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) to remove 2,4-dicholorophenol (2,4-DCP) under sunlight irradiation. The composite loaded with 50 wt% of FN-CQD removed 100 % of 2,4-DCP in 75 min, which was almost 2 times higher than g-C3N4. The photocatalytic performance was in agreement with ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) in which the photosensitizing effect was significantly exerted by 50 wt% of CQDs. In the presence of FN-CQDs, g-C3N4 was sensitized by sunlight with an average light intensity of ∼ 937 × 100 lx to donate more electrons for the generation of oxidizing radicals. Excessive loading of FN-CQDs (up to 50 wt%) created trap state that decreased the charge carrier transport in FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50). Such drawbacks did not affect the overall performance of FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50). The higher loading of FN-CQDs exerted stronger photosensitizing effects to overcome the limitation of high recombination rate of charge carriers and lower surface area. FN-CQDs could act as photosensitizer to increase the light absorption range to generate more electron and holes. It also served as electron reservoir for the reduction of oxygen molecule to produce superoxide anion radical (
O2 −). Scavenging tests identified that
O2 − was the most active radical in the photodegradation of 2,4-DCP.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper (Cu2+) ions are one of the most hazardous and recalcitrant heavy metal ions pollutants in untreated industrial effluents. The imbalanced concentration of Cu2+ ions in the human body upon exposure to pollutants could result in Menkes disease, Parkinson’s disease, Alzheimer’s disease and Wilson’s disease [1]. Therefore, an effective analytical method for sensitive and selective Cu2+ ions detection is important for pollutant identification. Fluorescence detection tools can be miniaturized at a high sensitivity of parts per billion or trillion with inexpensive and easy procedures. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are designed in fluorescence detection tools as they can be excited with a single excitation source for multiplexed detection compared to traditional dye techniques [2]. CQDs also possess other distinctive properties such as low toxicity, low cost, biocompatible, unique optical and electronic properties, and chemically inert with broad and tunable excitation spectrum due to its quantum sized [3–5]. Hence, CQDs were used as fluorophores to detect heavy metal ions in untreated industrial effluents. Jiao et al. utilized the mango peel derived CQDs for the detection of Fe2+ in ferrous succinate tablets with the linearity range of 4 μM–16 μM [6]. Liu et al. reported CQDs modified with lysine and bovine serum albumin, which selectively detect Cu2+ in tap water [7]. Pramanik et al. developed CQDs by hydrothermal carbonization of Aegle Marmelos leave powder which could be applied as a fluorescence Fe3+ ion sensor [8]. In this study, human fingernails were used as the green precursor for the hydrothermal synthesis of water-soluble and fluorescent CQDs using a top-down approach [9]. The fingernails derived CQDs (FN-CQDs) served as an effective fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+.

Additionally, the endocrine disrupting chemicals (EDCs) present in untreated industrial effluents such as 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP) could induce faint, itch, anemia and phospholipid bilayer disruption in the human cell by altering endocrine system function [10,11]. The 2,4-DCP was also reported to destroy the soil microbiota due to its toxicity through organic matter and clay dissociation [12]. However, their removal was difficult as they were stable in the molecular structure. Hence, the photocatalytic process was suggested to remove 2,4-DCP since the photocatalytic process utilized synergistic light and catalyst effect to degrade recalcitrant contaminants into harmless products. Among the photocatalysts, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) shines as a popular metal-free polymeric semiconducting compound due to its low band gap energy (2.7 eV), high thermal stability, non-toxicity and low cost [13]. Yet, the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 is limited by its short light absorption range, slow charge mobility and fast recombination of electron and hole pairs [14]. The shortcomings of g-C3N4 could be reduced by coupling with various semiconductors (TiO2 and BiVO4) [15,16] and coupling with metals (gold and silver) [17,18]. Jiang et al. reported a combination of N self-doping and thermal exfoliation process, in which the porous nitrogen self-doped g-C3N4 demonstrated 81.72 % of tetracycline degradation in 60 min [19]. Wang et al. (2019) combined g-C3N4 with 3D TiO2 microflowers to form direct Z-scheme heterojunction in the interface, leading to excellent charge separation and transportation [20]. Dang et al. loaded gold nanoparticles onto g-C3N4 nanosheet, facilitating the charge carrier separation and eventually improving the photoactivity for H2 evolution by 5.3 times higher than pure g-C3N4 [17]. He et al. prepared Bi2O3/g-C3N4 direct Z-scheme photocatalyst via the combination of photoreduction and subsequent oxidation by air. The composite showed improved photodegradation of phenol than pure Bi2O3 and g-C3N4 due to the Z-scheme charge transfer [21]. Despite recording positive properties and results, chemical-based doping agents are harmful to our environment and health whereas the noble metal doping is relatively more costly. As an alternative, carbon-based material such as CQDs is derived from a cheaper and greener precursor. Despite extensive research, few discernments are yet to be discovered to improve past studies’ data. This includes the application of CQDs derived from human fingernails in Cu2+ sensing and photocatalysis. Many works mainly focused on the applications of CQDs/g-C3N4 for photocatalytic water splitting and photodegradation of organic dye. The outcome is positive but there has been a little attempt on the photodegradation of EDCs using CQDs/g-C3N4. In this study, our group coupled g-C3N4 with FN-CQDs to enhance the photocatalytic performance. We further explored the metal sensing application in terms of selectivity and sensitivity of the prepared FN-CQDs towards various heavy metals.

Human fingernails were collected from students from SJK(c) Man Ming Gopeng primary school in Perak, Malaysia. The grinded fingernails (1 g) were added into 15 mL of ultrapure water. Next, the mixture was sealed into 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and further heated in the oven for 3 h at 200 °C. The FN-CQDs solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min and vacuum dried at 60 °C for 48 h. To prepare pure g-C3N4, urea (99.8 %, R&M chemicals) was dried in an oven for 24 h at 60 °C and then heated in the furnace by covering it in a crucible at 550 °C for 2 h. As synthesized pale yellowish g-C3N4 (1 g) was mixed with different weight percentages of FN-CQDs (30 wt%, 40 wt% and 50 wt%) and stirred for 24 h. The mixture was then sealed to autoclave and heated at 100 °C for 2 h. The composite residues were centrifuged and dried at 60 °C. The obtained composites are named as FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(30), FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(40) and FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50), respectively.

The morphology and composition of g-C3N4 and FN-CQDs/g-C3N4 were observed via a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Auriga, Zeiss) equipped with electron dispersive X-ray (EDX). High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM, FEI-TECNAI F20) images were obtained at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (FTIR, Perkin Elmer Spectrum 400 Spectrophotometer) were acquired in the wavenumber ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1 at resolution of 2 cm−1. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, PANalytical-Empyrean) was conducted to identify the crystalline phase of the composite. UV–vis absorbance and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CQDs solution were analyzed using Hach DR6000 UV–vis spectrophotometer and Perkin Elmer LS 55 PL spectrophotometer (λex =325 nm). The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) was obtained using UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu) equipped with an integrating sphere attachment using the BaSO4 as a reference. Time Resolved Photoluminescence (TRPL) spectrum was generated by Edinburgh FLS 920 using 376.4 nm picoseconds pulsed laser as laser source (λex =480 nm). Transient photocurrent response was obtained with 10 s intervals on an Ivium Tech Vertex potentiostat with Newport AM 1.5 G solar simulator as light source. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI Quantera II, Ulvac-PHI, Inc.) was used to further clarify the existing functional groups with Al Kα radiation source. The specific surface area was analyzed by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller using Micromeritics ASAP 2020 N2 adsorption equipment.

For metal ions detection application, FN-CQDs aqueous solution (0.1 g/L) and 1 mM concentration of metal ions stock solutions (Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Sn2+ and Sr2+ ions) were prepared. The precursors for Ba2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Mg2+ and Pb2+ such as barium nitrate (Ba(NO3)2, 99.9 %), cadmium nitrate (Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, 98 %), iron (III) sulphate (Fe2(SO4)3·xH2O, 97 %), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 98 %) and lead (II) nitrate (Pb(NO3)2, 99.9 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.9 %) and mercury (II) sulphate (HgSO4, 99.9 %) from Gene Chemical was used as a source for respective Ag+ and Hg2+. Precursors for Al3+, Mg2+ and Sn2+ such as aluminium chloride (AlCl3, 98 %), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 98 %) and tin (II) chloride dehydrate (SnCl2·2H2O, 99.9 %) were purchased from Merck. Copper (II) sulphate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2, 98 %) and strontium nitrate (Sr(NO3)2, 98 %) purchased from HmbG Chemicals was used as the precursor for respective Cu2+ and Sr2+. Iron (II) sulphate (FeSO4·7H2O, 99.5 %) and sodium sulphate (Na2SO4, 99 %) obtained from QRec was used as a precursor for Fe2+ and Na+. Potassium iodide (KI, 99.5 %) from ChemSoln was used as K+ source. A drop of metal ion solution was added with FN-CQDs aqueous solution and the fluorescent emission spectra were recorded using Perkin Elmer LS 55 PL spectrophotometer from 300 nm to 800 nm for each metal ion type. The excitation wavelength was set at 360 nm in room temperature. To evaluate the sensing system’s feasibility for the ions, the experiment was repeated with Cu2+ ions solution (FN-CQDs) by varying its concentration from 0 to 1000 nM under similar conditions.

The composite (0.05 g) was added into the 2,4-DCP solution (10 mg/L). The mixture was magnetically stirred in dark condition for 2 h to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium before photocatalysis. The photocatalytic experiment was carried out under the clear sky and sunny condition at Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman, Perak at the longitude 4.3394 °N and latitude 101.1398 °E between 11.00 a.m. and 1.00 pm on February 2020. The sunlight intensity was measured by using Light meter HS1010A of 2000 × 100 lx. Table S1 presents the light intensity recorded during the photocatalytic experiment. The degraded product was collected at every 15 min interval for 2 h. The 2,4-DCP concentration was analyzed with high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Agilent) equipped with a fluorescence detector and Ascentis C18 column (Supelco Analytical, USA). Acetonitrile (ACN) and water at 80:20 ratio was utilized as a mobile phase with a 0.8 mL/min flow rate. The concentration of 2,4-DCP was analyzed at retention time of 2.8 min and wavelength of 280 nm using a UV detector.

The photocatalytic degradation experiment was repeated with the addition of 2 mM of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na+) as h+ scavenger. Meanwhile, 2 mM of isopropyl alcohol (IPA), dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) and benzoquinone (BQ) act as a scavenger for
OH, e− and
O2 −, respectively. The experiment was conducted by using FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50). Table S2 shows the recorded light intensity during the radical scavenger experiment.

A preliminary experiment was done to identify the suitable loading range of FN-CQDs onto pure g-C3N4. As shown in Fig. S1, the loading of FN-CQDs in the range of 1–20 wt% did not exert significant improvement on the photocatalytic performance. It was reported that higher CQDs loading could benefit the photocatalytic performance as the CQDs act as a photosensitizer and electron trap site. The CQDs loading also increases the light harvesting region and lifespan of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs [22,23]. The preliminary results suggest that the loading of FN-CQDs could be optimized at a higher value and thus the loading range was advanced to 30, 40 and 50 wt% to confirm the hypothesis.

Fig. 1 a displays that the irregular lamellar structure of pure g-C3N4 was distributed in a non-uniform manner. The surface of FN-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites was found as agglomerated and wrinkled form after incorporating with FN-CQDs in Fig. 1(b–d). It was demonstrated that the morphological structures became more packed and aggregated as the concentration of FN-CQDs increased in the composites. These results were consistent with Sim et al. study which suggested that higher concentration of CQDs created a more clustered effect in the composites via the self-assembly process in hydrothermal treatment [24]. The EDX analysis displayed in the inset of Fig. 1b established the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O) elements in FN-CQDs/g-C3N4 composite. The C and N elements originated from g-C3N4 were detected in both pristine g-C3N4 (Fig. S2) and FN-CQDs/g-C3N4, whereas the presence of O element was attributed to the reaction between urea and oxygen during the fabrication of g-C3N4 under ambient temperature.

The TEM and HRTEM images of FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) are shown in Fig. 1(e–f). The TEM images of the composites demonstrate that FN-CQDs were evenly distributed on the surface of the g-C3N4 lamellar structure. The particle size of FN-CQDs (dark spots) ranges from 1.96 nm to 4.15 nm as presented in the inset of Fig. 1e. The average particle size of FN-CQDs was measured to be 3.10 nm, proving that the FN-CQDs were successfully synthesized in nanosized. The particle size of photocatalyst below 20 nm was able to hamper the recombination rate of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs and promote visible light harvesting effect [25]. As shown in Fig. 1f, similar lattice fringes spacing of 0.21 nm was found in FN-CQDs corresponding to (1 1 0) plane in FN-CQDs (JCPDS 89-0440). This result was consistent with the study stated by Tang et al. (2012) [26].

The FTIR of human fingernails was shown in Fig. 2 a. The broad peak at 3281 cm−1 and 2959 cm-1 was accredited to the absorption bands of OH of carboxylic group and CH group respectively. The peaks at 1642 cm−1 and1535 cm−1were observed as amide I and amide III bands of α-helical protein structure found in fingernails [27]. For FN-CQDs, the peak at 3222 cm-1 was assigned to the absorption bands of OH which confirmed the presence of carboxylic group. The absorption band at 2959 cm-1 was assigned to the bending vibrations of CH groups [28]. Other major peaks at 1643 cm-1, 1535 cm-1, 1387 cm-1 and 616 cm−1 from FN-CQDs were attributed to CO, NH, CC and CH, respectively [
29,30]. As shown in Fig. 2b, amide NH stretching is represented by the band located at 3083 cm-1 and 3227 cm-1 while the stretching vibration of OH functional group is represented by the band located at 3400 cm-1 [31–33]. The sp3 C–N and sp2 CN stretching vibrations indicate CN heterocycles in the range of 1638 cm−1 to 1237 cm−1 [34]. The obvious peak located at 812 cm−1 was matched to the CH bond (trisubstituted) in FN-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites. It was deduced that the addition of FN-CQDs did not affect the structure of g-C3N4.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, pure g-C3N4 demonstrated a small peak at 12.8° which corresponded to in-plane tri-s-triazine basic units structural packing within g-C3N4 sheets with (1 0 0) diffraction [35]. Another major peak at 27.6° indicated the aromatic compound C–N unit indexed as (0 0 2) peak for graphitic materials [36]. Additionally, a peak at 27.9° was observed in FN-CQDs which corresponded to amorphous nature of CQDs’ graphitic structure. After incorporating FN-CQDs into g-C3N4, the diffraction angle of FN-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites shifted from 27.6° to 27.9° with the increasing amount of FN-CQDs up to 50 wt% (Fig. 3a). This shifting process was ascribed to the formation of structural defects and lattice distance augments, suggesting that FN-CQDs were successfully introduced into the g-C3N4 host matrix. With the increased loading amount of FN-CQDs, the intensities of (1 0 0) and (0 0 2) diffraction peaks in the composites decreased. This indicates that the presence of FN-CQDs prevented the polymerization of dicyandiamide and the stacking (crystallization) of g-C3N4 sheets [37]. In FN-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites, there was no additional peak detected for FN-CQDs due to their non-crystalline structure [38].

Fig. S3 displays the UV–vis absorption spectrum of FN-CQDs aqueous solution from 200 to 800 nm. The apparent absorption peak at 301 nm and 371 nm was ascribed to the n-π* transition of CO bonds in FN-CQDs [39,40]. These excitation peaks were also ascribed as high energy level and complicated band structure in FN-CQDs product [41]. As shown in the inset of Fig. S3, FN-CQDs aqueous solution was brown colour under daylight and emitted a bright blue-green fluorescence colour upon UV light excitation at 365 nm. This proves that FN-CQDs were able to emit strong UV light upon excitation. Fig. 3b illustrates the PL emission behaviour of FN-CQDs across the excitation wavelength ranging from 320 to 540 nm. PL analysis revealed the maximum emission of FN-CQDs when excited at a certain wavelength [42]. The optimum excitation wavelength ranged from 320 to 380 nm while the optimal emission wavelength was set from 425 to 475 nm. The PL results demonstrate red-shifted wavelengths with decreasing emission intensity similar to the previous study by Martindale et al. [43]. This excitation-wavelength dependent behavior was mainly attributed to recombination of photogenerated charge carriers at surface-confined defect states and varied emissive trap site distribution. As the derivation of PL luminescence in FN-CQDs was not completely understood, the excitation-dependent PL behaviour could be interrelated to multiple distributions of passivated FN-CQDs emissive site, varied FN-CQDs particle size distribution, and photogenerated charge carriers recombination at surface-confined defect states [44,45].

As shown in Table 1 , the specific surface area of g-C3N4, FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(30), FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(40), FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) was 74.799, 61.290, 55.830, and 55.528 m2/g, respectively. The composites’ specific surface area and pore volume reduced when g-C3N4 was loaded with an increasing amount of FN-CQDs. As a result, the pore size increased. This suggests that higher loading of FN-CQDs will occupy and aggregate the surface and the pores of g-C3N4 [50]. Low pore volume in a product was not desirable as it could not provide sufficient active sites and reduced mass transfer between pollutant and photocatalyst products [51].

Fig. 5 a shows that the TRPL decay spectra of all photocatalysts decayed exponentially. The charge carrier lifetime increased with the decreasing concentration of FN-CQDs, following the sequence of FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(30) (7.510 ns) > FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(40) (4.104 ns) > FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) (3.877 ns) > g-C3N4 (1.865 ns). FN- CQDs were favoured to be incorporated in pure g-C3N4 for the charge separation to prevent recombination of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs and further enhanced photocatalytic performance. The FN-CQDs loading was able to form a type II van der Waals heterojunction which allowed the interaction of π-π stacking that reduced the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers [52]. However, the excessive loading of FN-CQDs more than 30 wt% will form trap states at the composite surface interface, which caused higher frequency of trapping and thus, deteriorated the electron transport [53]. Thus, the charge carrier lifetime was ideally prolonged with 30 wt% of FN-CQDs.

The photocurrents of FN-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites were investigated to examine the separation efficiency of e−-h+ pairs. As shown in Fig. 5b, the transient photocurrent response for all samples showed increased current intensity upon light irradiation and subsequently decreased of current intensity at dark condition. These current intensity changes for all tested samples showed that the charge transport rates were fast [54]. The order of the photogenerated charge carriers separation efficiency was as follow: FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(30) > FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(40) > FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) > g-C3N4. This suggests that the FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(30) had the highest efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers separation and lifetime. The transient photocurrent response result verified the TRPL results which indicate that the increased loading of FN-CQDs into g-C3N4 resulted in a better separation of charge carriers.

Fig. 6 a shows the C1s spectrum deconvoluted into four peaks including CC (284.56 eV), CO (285.66 eV), CO (287.76 eV) and COOH (288.16 eV) for FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) [55]. The peak CC is a standard carbon specialized for the composite, whereas the peak NC = N is a typical signal for sp2 hybridized carbon which represented the backbone of the g-C3N4 [56]. The XPS results also signified the interaction between FN-CQDs and g-C3N4 that resulted in abundant carbonyl and carboxyl groups. Fig. 6b displays peaks of three obvious N1s spectrums at 398.26 eV, 399.66 eV and 400.86 eV. The strong peak at 399.66 eV was attributed to typical sp2 hybridized triazine aromatic N units (C = NC) bonded to carbon atom [24]. Additionally, another peak at 398.26 eV was ascribed to tertiary N-(C)3 groups while the peak at 400.86 eV represented the amino functional C-N-H groups in FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) [57]. The O1s XPS spectrum in Fig. 6c displayed one spectrum with binding energy at 531.26 eV ascribed to the absorbed water in the composite. This XPS results proposed stacking interaction between FN-CQDs and g-C3N4 for their assembly due to non-covalent interactions prevented the formation of junction interface defects and ultimately, allowing good quality contact interface [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of FN-QDs in metal sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
The presence of a surface functional group of carboxylate and amino groups as proved in FTIR and XPS spectrums in FN-CQDs acted as chelating ligands for metal sensing applications. These chelating ligands react with trace metal ion to form metal ion complexes which changed their emissive states and PL intensity in the composites and thus, allowed the detection of specific trace metal ions [58]. Fig. 7 a shows that FN-CQDs had selective fluorescence response towards Cu2+ ions. The fluorescence of FN-CQDs that was quenched at the lowest intensity among all metal ions was reported to have fluorescence response from FN-CQDs. In order to evaluate the sensitivity of FN-CQDs towards Cu2+ ions, the fluorescence emission analysis was done by adjusting the concentration of Cu2+ ions (0, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 nM). As presented in Fig. 7b, the PL intensity of FN-CQDs was quenched with an increasing concentration of Cu2+ ions. A linear correlation was observed between the quenching level, (Fo -F)/Fo , against the concentration of Cu2+ ions based on Eq. (1):
(1) (Fo- F) / Fo = 0.56306Ci
where Fo and F refer to fluorescence intensity of FN-CQDs solution with absence and the presence of Cu2+ ions. Ci refers to the concentration of Cu2+ ions. Fig. S4 shows a good linear relationship from 0 to 1 nM with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.98348. This indicates that the produced FN-CQDs are sensitive to detect Cu2+ ions as low as 1 nM concentration.

A negligible effect of photocatalytic activities occurred in the blank solution with the absence of photocatalyst (Fig. 8 a). This shows that 2,4-DCP had poor photosensitizing property under sunlight irradiation. All FN-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites exhibited improved photocatalytic activity in comparison to the pure g-C3N4. The FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) recorded the highest 2,4-DCP of 100 % in 75 min, followed by FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(40) (94.13 %), FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(30) (88.57 %) and g-C3N4 (60.52 %). The experimental data were fitted into the first order kinetic model to study the photocatalytic reaction kinetics of 2,4-DCP degradation. As shown in Table S3, FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) performed 2.64 times higher than pure g-C3N4 at similar operating conditions. Pure g-C3N4 showed poorer degradation performance and lower kinetic constant due to their slow charge mobility and fast recombination of photogenerated e− and h+ pairs.

Table 2 shows that the introduction of FN-CQDs into the composites exerted positive improvement to the visible light harvesting property and separation efficiency of charge carriers except for specific surface area. Among the composites, FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) achieved the highest photodegradation rate, mainly contributed by its excellent visible light absorption property which overcomes the limitation of short charge carriers lifetime. Charge carrier lifetime and visible light harvesting capability is the dominant factor contributing to the enhancement of photocatalytic performance in lower (30 wt%) and higher (40 and 50 wt%) amount of FN-CQDs, respectively. Liu et al. reported that a large surface area in a photocatalyst can construct an efficient Z-scheme photocatalyst for better separation and transport of photogenerated charge carriers [59]. The small specific surface area in FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) hindered the separation and transport of photogenerated charge carriers and allowed the visible light absorption to be the dominant factor for the enhancement of photocatalytic efficiency. Fig. 8b shows the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of 2,4-DCP was heavily inhibited to 22.23 % upon adding BQ indicator into the mixture solution for FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50). The radical scavenging results show that
O2 − was the principal reactive radical species in this photocatalysis process as BQ inhibited the photocatalytic performance. Other reactive radical species such as h+,
OH and e− affected the photocatalytic degradation of 2,4-DCP at a minor level compared to
O2 − radicals. The significance of reactive radicals responsible for photocatalytic degradation of 2,4-DCP under sunlight irradiation followed the sequence of
O2 − > h+ >
OH > e.

The CB and VB edge potential in a semiconductor at zero charge point was calculated via Eqs. (2) and (3) [60]:
(2) EVB = X – EC + 0.5Eg
(3) ECB = EVB - Eg
where EVB and ECB are VB and CB edge potential, respectively; X is semiconductor electronegativity; Ec is energy of free e− on the H scale (∼4.5 eV vs. Normal Hydrogen Electrode (NHE)); and Eg is band gap energy of the semiconductor [60]. The g-C3N4 was reported to have X value of 4.64 eV [61]. The band gap energy (Eg) of g-C3N4 is 2.97 eV. Therefore, the VB and CB of g-C3N4 were measured at 1.63 eV and −1.34 eV, respectively.

The degradation mechanism of 2,4-DCP was correlated to the characterization results and proposed in Fig. 8c. The Eg of both pure g-C3N4 and composites was calculated in the range of 2.88–2.97 eV which enabled the photoexcited e− migrated from VB to CB of g-C3N4 and left photogenerated h+ in VB under the sunlight irradiation. The oxygen molecule dissolved in the 2,4-DCP aqueous solution was reduced by e− in the CB of g-C3N4 to obtain
O2 −. This is because the ECB of g-C3N4 (−1.34 eV) was more negative than the reduction potential of O2/
O2 − (−0.33 eV vs. NHE) [62]. In the VB, the 2,4-DCP might be directly oxidized by h+. However, the remaining h+ could not react with H2O and hydroxide (OH−) to form
OH radical. This is because the calculated VB of g-C3N4 (+1.63 eV) was less positive than the H2O/
OH (+2.38 eV vs. NHE) and OH−/
OH (+1.99 eV vs. NHE) [63]. The proposed mechanism is in agreement with the results of the scavenging experiment, which previously indicated that
O2 − radical is the main reactive species in the 2,4-DCP degradation. The generated
O2 − radicals effectively degraded 2,4-DCP solution to CO2 and water. The π conjugated FN-CQDs act as a photosensitizer to sensitize g-C3N4 and donate electrons to the CB of g-C3N4 [64]. Hence, g-C3N4 could extend its light absorption capacity towards the entire solar spectrum to produce more electrons and hole pairs. Meanwhile, the conducting texture of FN-CQDs in the system also acted as an e− reservoir to trap the e− injected from CB of g-C3N4 and retarded the recombination rate of e−-h+ pairs [65]. All these unique properties of the composites facilitated the photogeneration of charge carrier and electron transport at composite interface, accelerating the photocatalytic degradation of 2,4-DCP under sunlight exposure.

This present study demonstrates 100 % of 2,4-DCP removal in 75 min by FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50). A comparison between this study and previous researches is summarized in Table 3 . Other CQDs coupled photocatalysts such as bismuth(III) oxybromide (BiOBr), bismuth oxide with surface oxygen vacancy (Bi2O3-x), ZnO and bismuth oxide (α-Bi2O3) had a lower photocatalytic degradation efficiency compared to that of g-C3N4 [66–69]. These composites could not reduce the recombination of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs at a greater intensity than the g-C3N4 composite. Fang et al. reported that the CQDs facilitated the e− transfer from CB of g-C3N4 to O2 by increasing the surface photovoltage spectroscopy (SPS) signals of the photocatalyst which signified recombination reduction [37]. These SPS signals can be formed with the development of heterojunctions where interfacial electric fields were able to separate photogenerated charge carriers in opposite directions [70].

FN-CQDs were able to sense metal ions and perform photocatalytic activities upon impregnation of g-C3N4. The FESEM images revealed a non-uniform lamellar structure of pure g-C3N4 and clustered effect in the composites with the increased loading of FN-CQDs. The synthesized particle size of FN-CQDs was found in the range from 1.72 to 5.85 nm. The lattice fringes of 0.21 nm found in the composite of FN-CQDs/g-C3N4 corresponded to (1 1 0) plane of FN-CQDs. The introduction of FN-CQDs into g-C3N4 did not significantly affect on the basic structure of g-C3N4 in which the functional groups of amide, carboxylic, CN heterocycles and CH bond (trisubstituted) was still found in the composites. The loading of FN-CQDs created structural defects and lattice distance augments in the composites which shifted the diffraction peak assigned for aromatic compound CN unit of graphitic materials from 27.6° to 27.9°. The loading of 50 wt% FN-CQDs showed the best light absorption capability in the entire spectrum and a narrower band gap of 2.88 eV. The charge carrier lifetime of pure g-C3N4 was ideally prolonged from 1.865 ns to 7.510 ns with the loading amount of 30 wt% FN-CQDs. The excessive loading of FN-CQDs more than 30 wt% could form trap states at the composite surface interface, which caused the recombination of electron-hole pairs. In metal sensing experiments, the FN-CQDs were more selective towards the detection of Cu2+ ions and were sensitive to heavy metal concentrations as low as 1 nM with a linear correlation of 0.98348. Despite the decreased surface area from 74.799 m2/g to 55.528 m2/g after loading with FN-CQDs, it did not affect the performance of all composites which showed a higher degradation rate (100 %) than pure g-C3N4 (60.52 %). Full degradation of 2,4-DCP was exhibited by FN-CQDs/g-C3N4(50) in 75 min. The
O2 − radical was determined to be the principal reactive radicals in sunlight driven photocatalytic mechanism. Overall, the enhanced photodegradation performance of FN-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites was mainly due to the improved visible light harvesting and charge carrier lifetime. The results suggested that bioinspired FN-CQDs were able to serve as an excellent multipurpose sensing fluorescent tool with the photocatalytic application when paired with g-C3N4 under sunlight irradiation. This work will encourage novel developments in bioinspired CQDs for multipurpose applications of metal sensing and photocatalysis.

Jun Yan Tai: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Kah Hon Leong: Resources, Supervision. Pichiah Saravanan: Visualization, Validation. Sin Tee Tan: Resources, Methodology. Woon Chan Chong: Validation. Lan Ching Sim: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Visualization.
